text: The surgeon begins the prostatectomy by identifying the pubic bone as the first landmark. Using endoscopic visualization, the surgeon applies traction to expose the lateral side of the umbilical artery and opens it up. The vas deferens is visible and is taken down without complications.

The surgeon carefully incises the endopelvic fascia to better visualize the border of the prostate. The bladder is emptied to create more space in the surgical field. The surgeon isolates the dorsal vein, avoiding distal retraction to prevent bleeding. The periprostatic fat is identified and will be sent for final pathology analysis.

The surgeon incises the endopelvic fascia and applies traction to identify the best plane for nerve sparing. A minor incision is made to confirm the correct plane, followed by gentle pushing of muscle fibers to reveal the shape and distal end of the small prostate.

The surgeon uses traction and a Maryland dissector to spread tissue on the non-nerve-sparing side, revealing levator fibers and identifying the base of the prostate. The incision through the endopelvic fascia is visible, and the surgeon prepares to open the bladder, noting the detrusor fibers that guide the entry point for the bladder neck.

The surgeon opens the bladder and manipulates the catheter, pulling it back slightly to grab the tip and then pushing it in again. The bladder neck is visible, and the surgeon follows the shape of the prostate, ensuring not to thin the bladder wall. The interior of the bladder is inspected, noting the distance to the orifices.

The surgeon uses the ProGrasp instrument to manipulate the catheter and applies traction to visualize the detrusor fibers. The bladder wall thickness is checked, and urine is drained. The surgeon identifies the correct path to avoid entering the prostate, noting a bleeder and applying suction while using the Maryland for counter traction.

The surgeon isolates one of the vas deferens and uses it to lift up the structure, placing two hemologs on the bladder neck to open the space further. The perivesical fat and prostate are visible, with an open vessel nearby. The surgeon repeats the procedure on the left side, exposing the seminal vesicle and ensuring no vessels are present. The suction is applied to the vas deferens to manage any potential bleeding.

The surgeon clips a larger vessel near the bladder neck using hemologs, ensuring it is not too close to the bladder neck. The denovus fascia covering the seminal vesicle is visible, and the surgeon pushes it to the midline to identify the plane of the fascia. The fascia is cut and isolated further, with small clips applied to larger vessels for safety. The edge of the Danovese fascia is peeled off from the seminal vesicle, confirming the correct plane for dissection.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection underneath the prostate, lifting it slightly and using scissors to identify the intrafascial plane on the right side. The periprostatic fat is visible, and the surgeon aims to maintain precision with small clips during the dissection.

The surgeon lifts the prostate to follow the cleavage plane using a posterior approach, preparing the vessels for clipping. A small blue clip is placed to address minor oozing, while maintaining awareness of the high anterior anatomy to release the prostate at the base.

The surgeon identifies the lateral fascia covering the nerves and carefully pushes it away to expose small vessels. The approach is adjusted to navigate sticky tissue, and the position of the prostate is occasionally changed to facilitate access.

The surgeon identifies the prostate capsule and pushes surrounding fibers away, following the posterior approach to maintain the correct dissection plane. A blue clip is applied to secure tissue, and the prostate is repositioned slightly to ensure accurate anatomical orientation.

The surgeon exposes the apex of the prostate and gently pushes the neurovascular bundles away to reveal the prostate capsule. A clip is placed near the anastomosis site, with the urethra visible starting at the apex. The surgeon checks the midline to ensure proper treatment of the prostate on the rectal side, using scissors to gently open and push the tissue without rupturing it. The approach on this side involves a non-nerve sparing procedure, utilizing hemolog clips and electrocautery to manage the surrounding fat tissue.

The surgeon uses an extra-facial approach, leaving some tissue on the prostate. The denovus fascia is visible, and a clip is placed down near one of the arteries. The surgeon is careful not to stray too far from the prostate, noting the presence of fatty tissue and preparing to approach the apex.

The surgeon inserts a catheter to visualize its tip, revealing the fibers surrounding the prostate. Using a Maryland forceps, the surgeon manipulates the prostate, identifying vessels to be taken. The apex of the prostate is slowly developed, entering the centaurine fascia and plexus.

The surgeon identifies the correct plane for dissecting the urethra, moving away from the apex on one side. Muscle fibers are visible, appearing whitish, marking the border to the prostate. The surgeon takes out the tissue at the end of the prostate, encountering some arterial bleeding, which is controlled. The Santorini's plexus is freed, and the neurovascular bundle is dissected using cautery, with no preservation needed in this area. The prostate is removed, revealing pure fat and no nerves in the rectolateral aspect.

The surgeon is in the apical region, cutting through the urethra and seminal vesicle area. The nerves are detached, and the surgeon is focusing on covering the entire area. The inner side of the tissue is yellow, and the outer side is marked blue for orientation. The surgeon discusses the importance of identifying cancer presence on the blue side for pathology analysis.
The surgeon sutures the dorsal vein complex using Vicryl sutures, while adjusting the position of the third robotic arm to achieve a better angle in the narrow pelvic space.

The surgeon performs a Rocco suture using a monofilic monocryal suture, adapting the bladder with two layers. The first layer moves the bladder down, while the second layer grabs the mucosa. The posterior layer is secured with a good bite, and the ilial conduct is mobilized for the right urethral dissection, prepared with a handmade sling.

The surgeon manipulates the Denonvilliers fascia, using a Maryland dissector to grasp and move the tissue underneath the urethra. A clip is prepared to secure the fascia, preventing it from sliding back. The surgical field is reduced to stop minor bleeding, and a small vessel is clipped to control oozing. The surgeon inspects for bleeding before completing the Rocco suture.

The surgeon manipulates the bladder neck, which has a small opening, by lifting the catheter to expose the retracted mucosa. In the narrow pelvis, adjustments are made to avoid collision with the pelvic walls. The mucosa is carefully aligned, ensuring the edges touch without closing the space too much, while managing minor oozing with sutures.
The surgeon uses a barbed suture to tie a knot, ensuring good resistance in the midline during the prostatectomy procedure.

The surgeon carefully avoids incorporating the sphincter muscle into the suture line during the anastomosis, aiming to preserve the functional tissue. The mucosa and bladder neck are visible, and the surgeon ensures that the muscle is not included in the suture to potentially improve early continence.

The surgeon grabs the sutured dorsal vein complex and inserts the final catheter, ensuring no traction on the anastomosis. The catheter is blocked, and the bladder is filled with a syringe to check for leaks. Upon filling, a leakage is observed on the lateral side, indicated by visible bubbles.

The surgeon identifies a leakage and performs a suture to address it, ensuring the thread is tied securely. A small vessel is visible and managed during the procedure. The surgeon prepares to dissect the lymph nodes using scissors on arm three.

The surgeon follows the ureter down, opening the tissue further and using two hemlock clips to secure vessels. The laparoscopic transperitoneal approach is used to access the retroperitoneal space, with care taken to preserve the nerve while managing accompanying vessels.

The surgeon identifies the urethra, gonadal vein, and thickened lymphatic tissue. Methylene blue is injected into the left inguinal lymph node, and leakage is observed in the retroperitoneum four minutes later. The ureter is visible as the surgical field is opened further.

The surgeon carefully identifies the ureter and avoids grabbing it, pushing it aside to expose the internal iliac region. The lymph nodes are visible and ready for removal, with the genital femoral nerve serving as a landmark to avoid lateral dissection beyond this point.
The surgeon identifies an enlarged lymph node and carefully manipulates it during the lymphadenectomy procedure. The lymph node is not placed into a specimen bag for removal.

The surgeon navigates to the obturator fossa, ensuring the bladder is emptied for better visualization. A circumflex vessel is identified and preserved, while small clips are applied to adjacent vessels as needed.

The surgeon carefully maneuvers to remove tissue from the trocar, ensuring not to damage it while checking for bleeding along the side wall. The artery and vein are identified, with attention given to the ratus artery.

The surgeon inspects the area beneath the external structure for any remaining tissue, then proceeds to the left side to take the vas and incise the peritoneum. The nerve and the edge of the vein are visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon manipulates tissue around the ureter, which is visible and appears slim. The internal iliac artery is identified, and care is taken to avoid coagulation as the bowel is positioned right underneath.
The surgeon identifies a vessel and a nearby nerve, carefully preserving the vessel while monitoring the vein's entry point.
